title: Rooms By The Hour

jokes.text: Moira asks David if he has safety pins, then immediately pivots to 'your agent called'
jokes.setup: Moira enters and says 'your agent called' then immediately asks for safety pins — a bizarre non-sequitur ordering
jokes.punchline: The safety pins are somehow the more urgent ask despite the agent news
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira character comedy — her priorities are scrambled in a very specific Moira way. The safety pin ask before the agent news is perfectly in character but the joke lands softly as an opener.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Do I look like your agent?

jokes.text: 'Ginnifer? Or Jennifer with a G?' / 'There's more than one!'
jokes.setup: David asks which agent called, using the name Jennifer
jokes.punchline: Moira has multiple agents, and the distinction between 'Ginnifer' and 'Jennifer with a G' is treated as critical intelligence — then revealed there are multiple
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully layered Moira moment. The specificity of 'Ginnifer with a G' signals her world of showbiz pretension, and 'There's more than one!' escalates the absurdity by implying multiple agents named variants of Jennifer. Strong character integration — only Moira has this problem.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, I think you have a very serious medical condition.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's called narcissistic personality disorder, and it's terminal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, it's not. It's actually very sad. For everyone around you.

jokes.text: David's email inbox contains 'hot singles in my area' instead of the agent's message
jokes.setup: Moira searches David's email for the agent's message
jokes.punchline: The first thing she finds is 'hot singles in my area' spam
jokes.explanation: A reliable but not particularly original joke — spam email as character reveal. Works in context but doesn't add much. The beat earns a mild laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, I'm checking your email for important agent correspondence.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. It's just spam dating ads.

jokes.text: 'Attractive and spunky forty-something female.' / 'In my sleep!'
jokes.setup: David reads the casting description for the role
jokes.punchline: Moira confidently declares she could play 'spunky forty-something' in her sleep — no acknowledgment that 'forty-something' may not apply
jokes.explanation: Moira's shameless confidence in claiming a descriptor that clearly doesn't fully apply is perfect Moira character comedy. The brevity of 'In my sleep!' is the joke's engine. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It says 'must be willing to cry on command.' Can you cry on command?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, I've been crying on command since my second husband left me for his pilates instructor.

jokes.text: The film is 'The Crows Have Eyes... Two' — a sequel to a film no one has heard of
jokes.setup: Moira gets an audition for a psychological thriller called 'The Crows Have Eyes... Two'
jokes.punchline: The ellipsis in the title and the 'Two' reveal it's a sequel — treated as prestigious
jokes.explanation: The film title itself is a joke — the ellipsis in 'The Crows Have Eyes...' gives it false gravitas, and the 'Two' deflates it into bargain-bin territory. Economical and well-constructed absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The sequel is coming out next week. I'm very excited about it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's so cool, mom!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the first one was quite successful, so naturally they're capitalizing on that momentum.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've never actually seen the first one.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've never heard of it.

jokes.text: 'It's a sequel, that's good. It must mean the first one was a big success.' / 'I've never heard of it.'
jokes.setup: Moira reasons that being a sequel means the franchise is successful
jokes.punchline: David flatly notes he's never heard of the first one, undercutting her logic entirely
jokes.explanation: A tightly structured two-beat joke. Moira's faulty syllogism ('sequel = success') is immediately deflated by David's deadpan 'I've never heard of it.' Perfect economy, excellent structure. The straight-man timing is crisp.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, please. We've been through this. Every time you get offered a role, it's somehow going to require us to completely upend our lives.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But David, this could be the opportunity of a lifetime! A commercial for artisanal cheese!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a commercial. For cheese. And somehow I know this means I'll be driving you to auditions at 5 AM, you'll need a new headshot, and Patrick will have to reschedule his entire week.

jokes.text: Moira requests a drop cloth, a ring light, and then David suggests propping his phone against books
jokes.setup: Moira asks David for a camera, then escalates her production demands
jokes.punchline: David's suggestion to prop his phone against some books — the gap between Moira's cinematic expectations and David's minimal effort
jokes.explanation: The escalating list (camera → drop cloth → ring light) followed by David's deflationary 'prop my phone against some books' is a nice class/effort gap joke. Moira's diva demands vs. David's laziness is well-mined territory for them.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm doing fine. I've got everything under control. I know exactly what I'm doing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the system here is very... it's complicated. Very complicated. Not like other agencies.

jokes.text: 'And so it begins again.' — David's weary reaction to being drafted into Moira's productions
jokes.setup: Moira is excitedly marshaling resources for her self-tape
jokes.punchline: David's resigned, world-weary aside — implying a long history of being conscripted into Moira's theatrical endeavors
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — four words that contain an entire backstory. David's aside works because it implies a lifetime of exactly this. The humor is in what's left unsaid. Strong character work.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: You seem like you're... struggling a bit.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I wouldn't say struggling, I'd say I'm... dealing with some challenges. Ish.

jokes.text: Johnny nervously fumbles with hotel check-in computer while trying to appear competent
jokes.setup: Johnny confidently tells a guest he can 'certainly handle this' on the computer his associate usually operates
jokes.punchline: The computer chimes, beeps, and clearly malfunctions as Johnny struggles — accompanied by his increasingly strained composure
jokes.explanation: A well-executed cringe sequence — the wealthy former mogul humbled by basic hotel software. The sound cues (chimes, beeps) amplify the physical comedy. Not groundbreaking but competently executed fish-out-of-water humor.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Wait, so there's no competition yet?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Not yet, dear. But I'm sure once word gets out that I'm auditioning, every actress in this town will be scrambling for the role.

jokes.text: 'Are you new at this?' / 'Ish.' (Laughs nervously)
jokes.setup: Johnny has been visibly struggling with the booking system
jokes.punchline: 'Ish' — a masterpiece of understatement for a billionaire reduced to struggling with motel software
jokes.explanation: 'Ish' is a perfect single-word joke. The entire setup of a formerly powerful man humbled by motel check-in software is resolved by one syllable delivered with a nervous laugh. Economy score is near-perfect — maximum comedy, minimum words. This is the kind of moment that demonstrates great comedic instincts.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, everyone knows this movie.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I don't.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: But congratulations.

jokes.text: 'I would love to pay cash.' / 'Whew! Even better.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is struggling with the computer booking system
jokes.punchline: The client's offer to pay cash is treated by Johnny as great news — but to the audience, cash-only payments for 'afternoon room rentals' is an obvious red flag he's missing
jokes.explanation: A beautifully layered dramatic irony joke — Johnny sees cash as a solution to his computer problems, but the audience immediately reads it as a massive red flag. His 'Even better!' is funnier because we know what he doesn't. Strong structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I'm just so honored to be taking your part. I mean, I know I'm not nearly as talented or sophisticated as you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Twyla, stop. I will not have you disparaging yourself in my presence. It's unseemly and frankly, it's giving very little to the room.

jokes.text: The other actress for the role: 'they haven't found her yet'
jokes.setup: Moira says it's between her and 'one other actress'
jokes.punchline: The other actress hasn't been found yet — so there's no competition, undermining the 'shortlisting'
jokes.explanation: A crisp joke that subtly deflates the prestige of Moira's 'shortlisting.' The audience understands immediately that being shortlisted against a nonexistent competitor isn't quite the honor Moira thinks it is. Economy and structure are strong.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, he looks like if Ryan Gosling and a German Shepherd had a baby.

jokes.text: 'I'm sure you've seen The Crows Have Eyes.' / 'I have never heard of it, but congratulations.'
jokes.setup: Moira promotes the film franchise with confidence
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn has never heard of it either — the running confirmation that this is an obscure project
jokes.explanation: A callback to David's earlier 'I've never heard of it' — now Jocelyn confirms the film's obscurity. The running gag works because Moira's obliviousness never wavers. 'But congratulations' is a lovely polite addition.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I mean, it's inappropriate. Using sex to sell veterinary services? It's exploitative and frankly, it cheapens the profession.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, you literally have a poster of that sexy vet in your office.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That's different. That's for... educational purposes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's literally covering your filing cabinet.

jokes.text: 'This would be the second in the franchise.'
jokes.setup: No one has heard of the first film
jokes.punchline: Moira doubles down by calling it a 'franchise' — implying empire-level prestige for an unheard-of bird horror film
jokes.explanation: Moira's insistence on calling this a 'franchise' is perfectly delusional. The word does heavy lifting — it implies Marvel-scale ambition for what is clearly a tiny, unknown production. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not just sitting around doing nothing. I'm actively working on my abs.

jokes.text: Moira's speech about self-belief: 'No deprecatory mocking. One must champion oneself and say, I am ready for this! Otherwise, the pressure could be crippling.'
jokes.setup: Twyla humbly says it would be big shoes to fill
jokes.punchline: Moira launches into an actorly pep talk that applies to herself as much as Twyla, delivered as grand wisdom from a woman who absolutely needs this advice
jokes.explanation: The projection in this speech is the joke — Moira is coaching herself through her own insecurities via Twyla. The language is gloriously Moira: 'deprecatory mocking,' 'champion oneself.' Solid character work.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I mean, if you're going to use sex to sell your business, why don't you just become a male stripper?

jokes.text: Patrick/Ted plot: 'He looks like if Ricky Martin and Mark Consuelos had a baby.'
jokes.setup: Alexis and Ted are looking at a competing vet's attractive marketing photo
jokes.punchline: Alexis's specific celebrity-baby mashup description of the competitor's appeal
jokes.explanation: A very Alexis line — the specificity of naming two similar-looking Latin heartthrobs as the celebrity mash-up is funny and totally in character for someone who thinks entirely in celebrity references. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What's the Dude Cave?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's where your dad hangs out. He's been working on his car down there.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, which car?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: The Cadillac. He says it's a classic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That car is older than me. Like, significantly.

jokes.text: 'It's the competition, and he's selling his body to get clients, it's disgusting!' — Ted's outraged reaction to a sexy vet photo
jokes.setup: A rival vet is using an attractive photo for marketing
jokes.punchline: Ted is outraged by the 'exploitation' while clearly being physically competitive himself
jokes.explanation: Ted's moral outrage about 'selling his body' is funny because we know he's attractive and it's setting up his own unconscious vanity. The irony is clearly intentional and it lands.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, it's mostly just lonely men watching a bunny eat lettuce for eight hours a day.

jokes.text: 'You could probably take just as sexy a photo if you wanted to.' / 'I could definitely take just a... I don't want to.'
jokes.setup: Alexis suggests Ted could compete on looks
jokes.punchline: Ted starts to enthusiastically agree before catching himself and insisting he doesn't want to
jokes.explanation: Ted's self-interruption is pitch-perfect. He almost completes 'I could definitely take just as sexy a photo' before his moral self-image kicks in and he reverses. The mid-sentence pivot is the joke — it reveals exactly what he was about to admit. Strong structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How do you want me to count you in?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, just say 'one, two, three, go.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay. One, two, three, go.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, that's not right. Try again.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: One, two, three, go.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You're saying the numbers too evenly. I need you to say 'one' like it's a question, 'two' like it's a statement, and then skip 'three' and go straight to 'four.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not how counting works.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it's how *I* count in.

jokes.text: 'And... it's not a full six yet, that's why I'm shredding right now.'
jokes.setup: Alexis notes Ted has a six pack, undermining his 'I don't care about looks' stance
jokes.punchline: Ted clarifies it's 'not a full six yet' and he's actively 'shredding' — a complete contradiction of his stated values
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best Ted joke. 'It's not a full six yet, that's why I'm shredding right now' perfectly demolishes his pretense of not caring about physical appearance. The specificity of 'not a full six yet' — counting his own abs in progress — and the casual revelation that he's actively working on it is a beautiful self-own that only this character could deliver. Strong across all craft dimensions.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A murder of crows.

jokes.text: 'If he wants to sell sex, then he should just get a job down at the Dude Cave!'
jokes.setup: Ted is still outraged by his competitor's attractive marketing
jokes.punchline: Ted recommends the competitor get a job at 'the Dude Cave' — a name that implies an all-male strip club, which Twyla immediately confirms
jokes.explanation: The name 'Dude Cave' is inherently funny, and Ted's moral indignation fueling a strip club recommendation is a great contradiction. The setup earns the punchline.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You realize this is just 'The Raven,' right?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, it's not. There are crows in it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There's one crow. In 'The Raven.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I have several.

jokes.text: 'The bottom line is that I did not go to med school just to exploit innocent puppies as a cover for my extreme vanity!'
jokes.setup: Alexis is pushing Ted to use his looks for marketing
jokes.punchline: Ted frames his objection in absurdly high-minded terms — 'exploit innocent puppies as a cover for my extreme vanity' — while the phrase 'my extreme vanity' is a surprising self-admission
jokes.explanation: The line is funny on multiple levels: the puppies as 'innocent' cover story, and his self-description of 'extreme vanity' — which he then immediately undermines by clarifying it's not a full six-pack yet. The self-awareness buried in moral outrage is well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would they name two characters the same thing? That's confusing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's not confusing, darling. One is pronounced differently.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're spelled the same way.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but the *intent* is different.

jokes.text: Bunny cam idea justified as 'very popular with people who hate their jobs, or are in loveless marriages'
jokes.setup: Alexis pitches a live bunny cam to drive website traffic
jokes.punchline: She identifies the target demographic as people who hate their jobs or are in loveless marriages — a cheerfully bleak audience description
jokes.explanation: A sharp observational joke about internet animal content — the brutal honesty of the target market is funny because it's true, and Alexis delivers it with zero ironic awareness. Good economy and an unexpected bit of social commentary from Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The logic of the script is actually quite sound.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, the main character's love interest is a sentient bottle of pinot grigio.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, and it works.

jokes.text: 'Are those the people we wanna be targeting?' / 'Well, it's free marketing, and you can find little homes for those little bunnies that don't have any houses.'
jokes.setup: Ted questions whether the miserable internet-browsing demographic is their target
jokes.punchline: Alexis's practical counter: free marketing, plus the bunnies need 'little homes' — the repetition of 'little' and 'houses' is cutely absurd
jokes.explanation: 'Bunnies that don't have any houses' is a gentle, slightly silly way to end the bunny cam pitch. The baby-talk phrasing is mildly amusing but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, you literally act with a corpse, so...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not sure what you're looking for from me here.

jokes.text: 'But if those bunnies feel exploited even a little bit, I am pulling the plug! / Yes. / Honeypea.'
jokes.setup: Ted reluctantly agrees to the bunny cam
jokes.punchline: His condition is that the bunnies must not feel exploited — anthropomorphizing rabbits — then 'Honeypea' as a pet name punctuates the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Ted's concern for the bunnies' emotional dignity is in perfect character — he's a vet who genuinely anthropomorphizes animals. 'Honeypea' as the tender nickname capper is sweet and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, I have been acting since before you were born. In fact, I have been acting since significantly before you were born.

jokes.text: Moira's counting process: 'Start from five, you leave out the two and the one.' / 'Why?' / 'Because it's my process.'
jokes.setup: David asks how to count Moira in for her audition tape
jokes.punchline: Moira's idiosyncratic count (5-4-skip-3-skip) is presented as a non-negotiable artistic process with no justification other than 'it's my process'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Moira jokes. The specificity of skipping only the 2 and the 1 (not the 3) is delightfully arbitrary, and 'because it's my process' delivered with total conviction captures Moira's theatrical mysticism perfectly. Only Moira would have a counting ritual this specific and this absurd. Strong character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, I don't appreciate you giving me notes on my performance. I am a trained professional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you were born three weeks early.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that explains it.

jokes.text: Moira performs the crow scene: 'They're watching you, Gareth. The crows have eyes. And you better not look them in it.'
jokes.setup: Moira performs her audition monologue from 'The Crows Have Eyes II'
jokes.punchline: 'You better not look them in it' — grammatically mangled dialogue delivered with full theatrical commitment
jokes.explanation: The dialogue is intentionally terrible — 'look them in it' instead of 'look them in the eye' — but Moira commits fully. The humor is in the gap between her total conviction and the script's badness. A great showcase for the character.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I've finally turned this business around. We're profitable, we're thriving, people actually respect what we've built here.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah. Cool.

jokes.text: 'And it's not a flock, my dear boy... it's a murder.' / 'Yes, I said murder. Gareth, murder!'
jokes.setup: Gareth calls a group of crows 'a flock'
jokes.punchline: Moira-as-Mandrake corrects him — a murder of crows — then over-explains with increasing drama as if 'murder' is a revolutionary concept
jokes.explanation: The joke plays on the double meaning of 'murder' (a group of crows / violent death) while also lampooning the overwrought dialogue. Moira's repetition of 'murder! Gareth, murder!' as if it's a revelation is excellent. The escalation within the monologue works well.

jokes.text: David critiques: 'This is just one big rip off of The Birds!'
jokes.setup: Moira has just performed her crow-horror audition piece with full conviction
jokes.punchline: David immediately punctures it by noting the obvious Hitchcock rip-off
jokes.explanation: David as the voice of reason puncturing Moira's theatrical bubble is a reliable dynamic. The Hitchcock comparison is an obvious joke but David's delivery makes it land. 'This is about crows! It's much more specific!' as Moira's counter is the better half.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm simply describing what I observed. A woman, providing companionship services, meeting gentlemen in hotel rooms for substantial fees, with a clear understanding that certain... physical favors would be exchanged for compensation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a legitimate business arrangement. She sets her rates, they negotiate the terms, and then they spend time together in a private setting where—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What? I'm just saying she's an entrepreneur. A very flexible entrepreneur who specializes in making men happy for money.

jokes.text: 'This is about crows! It's much more specific!'
jokes.setup: David accuses the script of ripping off The Birds
jokes.punchline: Moira defends it by insisting crows are more specific than birds generally — as if that's the meaningful distinction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira logic: the film is defensible because crows are a subset of birds, therefore it's original. The specificity defense of a clear rip-off is absurd and brilliantly Moira. Economy is near-perfect — two short sentences contain the entire joke.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That sounds like you're describing yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I'm aware.

jokes.text: 'Look, they've taken Clara!' / 'Where are they carrying her to, Clara?' / Are there two Claras?!
jokes.setup: David reads dialogue from the script featuring apparent narrative confusion
jokes.punchline: The script has a character named Clara asking where Clara is being taken — seemingly two different characters with the same name — which David finds absurd
jokes.explanation: A great absurdist joke about bad writing — the script appears to have both a Clara and a different Clara, which Moira dismisses with 'It's a common name.' The deadpan defense of catastrophically bad writing is the joke. David's exasperation is earned.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that a bunny? That's adorable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, why is someone filming a bunny 24 hours a day? That's not normal. Something's wrong. That bunny is definitely dead.

jokes.text: 'It's a common name.' — Moira defending two characters named Clara
jokes.setup: David points out the apparent confusion of two characters named Clara
jokes.punchline: Moira's utterly untroubled 'It's a common name' — as if this solves the problem
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — three words that perfectly capture Moira's willful blindness to problems. 'It's a common name' as a complete rebuttal to a fundamental script logic problem is a brilliant non-answer delivered with total conviction. One of the episode's sharpest lines.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is adorable. Wait, where is this going? Is this... is this going to end with the bunny being eaten by a hawk?

jokes.text: 'The next scene takes place in a large bird's nest.'
jokes.setup: The script analysis continues
jokes.punchline: David casually mentions a scene set in a large bird's nest as if it's the least surprising thing, which lands as another layer of the script's absurdity
jokes.explanation: A nice absurdist detail drop — 'a large bird's nest' as a setting is inherently ridiculous, and David's matter-of-fact delivery amplifies it. A supporting joke that builds the portrait of a terrible script.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The bunny cam has like, way more viewers than your Instagram.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, my Instagram is private, so...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's not private, David. I follow you, and I'm not even following you.

jokes.text: 'Rarely is the material of quality, the challenge is to lift it off the page.'
jokes.setup: David criticizes the script's quality
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots to performing-arts wisdom as a defense mechanism — the material is bad, but she as artist transcends it
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira — turning a damning critique into a showcase for her own artistry. 'Rarely is the material of quality' is a beautifully grandiose way to admit the script is trash while still insisting she's above it. Strong character voice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He's not undressing, Alexis. He's just... removing his clothes.

jokes.text: 'I'm getting nothing from you. It's like working off a corpse.'
jokes.setup: Moira blames David for the audition tape not going well
jokes.punchline: Accusing her scene partner (her son, who is not an actor) of being a 'corpse' to work off — as if he's a professional
jokes.explanation: Moira treating David as a professional scene partner and critiquing his performance is a funny inversion of normal family dynamics. The 'corpse' metaphor is appropriately dramatic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, did you see his body? It was amazing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, and I'm going to spend the rest of my life trying to unsee what I did see.

jokes.text: 'I actually think you do play off a corpse in the next scene.'
jokes.setup: Moira says working with David is like working off a corpse
jokes.punchline: David points out the next scene of the script actually features a literal corpse — so his being corpse-like is relevant practice
jokes.explanation: A brilliant callback that turns Moira's insult into an accidental compliment — David is literally preparing her for the next scene. The economy is excellent, the structure is tight, and it wrong-foots Moira completely. One of the episode's cleverest lines.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I understand you work in... a particular field of business.

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: Oh honey, I don't work in a field. I work in a lot of positions.

jokes.text: 'This is all playing a bit big.' / 'Hmph! Big.' / 'And would that be a note? A note, coming from my son?'
jokes.setup: David gently notes that Moira's performance is overplayed
jokes.punchline: Moira's indignant response — treating a casual note from her son as a professional affront, invoking her seven-month pregnancy and her TV movie credits as credentials
jokes.explanation: Moira invoking her pregnancy as an artistic credential is brilliant — 'who I carried for almost a full seven months' does double duty, both as proof of their bond and as evidence of her suffering-for-art. 'Almost a full seven months' is a funny detail that implies premature birth or she's fudging the number.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What exactly is it that you do?

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: I'm a sex worker, John. And I'm very good at what I do.

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: I have a very high client retention rate.

jokes.text: 'Who I carried for almost a full seven months, to an actress who has been short-listed for more than seven major made for television motion pictures?'
jokes.setup: Moira defends herself against David's direction note
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'made for television motion pictures' — TV movies — being called 'major' and 'motion pictures' by Moira, delivered as a credential
jokes.explanation: 'Made for television motion pictures' is a perfectly Moira phrase — she uses 'motion pictures' to dignify TV movies, and 'short-listed' implies she rarely got them. The self-aggrandizement that's actually self-undermining is peak Moira. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm concerned about what goes on in these rooms.

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: Well, people sleep in them, they shower, sometimes they have sex...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well—

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: It's a hotel room, Johnny. That's what they're for.

jokes.text: 'The script is nothing more than a jumping off point.' — Moira after reading terrible dialogue
jokes.setup: The script has been conclusively established as terrible
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes it as a creative springboard — 'jumping off point' — to avoid admitting it's bad
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira reframing — the worse the material, the more she wraps it in theatrical mysticism. 'Jumping off point' is a believable acting-class euphemism for 'this script is garbage.' Solid character work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Are you running a prostitution ring out of our home?

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: Oh honey, it's not a ring. It's more of a... loose network.

jokes.text: Moira attempts to count herself in but David's phone camera beeps and apparently dies before she gets through her count
jokes.setup: After all the drama, Moira declares she'll count herself in
jokes.punchline: She counts '5, 4, 3, 2...' and the camera immediately beeps — presumably dying or recording — undercutting the whole laboriously built-up process
jokes.explanation: A visual payoff to the counting saga — after all the ritual about the counting process, something goes wrong with the equipment anyway. The joke is in the futility of all that preparation.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me I've been on camera this whole time?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I literally just noticed.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Just noticed?! Alexis, I've been undressing for the past ten minutes!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, I was... multitasking.

jokes.text: Johnny brags about booking three rooms — 'afternoons only, Trish' — as if this is a triumph
jokes.setup: Johnny tells Stevie about the rooms he booked
jokes.punchline: His proud annotation 'afternoons only, Trish' in the booking is clearly a massive red flag he's presenting as evidence of good work
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony continues — Johnny is genuinely proud of the booking details that scream 'illicit activity' to everyone else. His obliviousness is the joke, and it's well-sustained dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, Ted was literally just standing there with his shirt off. Like, he wasn't even trying to hide it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Perhaps he was making a statement.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A statement? Mom, the only statement he was making was 'I have a body and I'm not afraid to show it.' Which, honestly, is kind of sexy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's really not.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I thought it was intentional. Like, who takes their shirt off that casually? He was obviously trying to seduce someone.

jokes.text: Johnny's defense of the rooms-by-hour situation: 'a group of business professionals decompressing for a few hours, during a long day at a regional accountants' conference'
jokes.setup: Stevie tells Johnny what the rooms are clearly being used for
jokes.punchline: Johnny constructs an increasingly elaborate and specific innocent explanation — 'regional accountants' conference' — that makes it sound even more absurd the more detail he adds
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'regional accountants' conference' as Johnny's constructed alibi is funny — he's trying to sound authoritative but the more details he adds, the more transparently he's in denial. Strong character work for Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, people are clearly interested in seeing Ted without his clothes on. I think that's pretty normal.

jokes.text: 'You don't hear it, when you say it like that?' / 'I hear it a bit.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has just described an elaborate innocent scenario for the afternoon room rentals
jokes.punchline: Stevie asks if he hears how absurd it sounds; Johnny concedes 'I hear it a bit' — admitting partial awareness while still not fully accepting reality
jokes.explanation: Four words — 'I hear it a bit' — that perfectly capture Johnny's psyche: a man of immense dignity who is slowly being forced to acknowledge his humbling situation. The partial concession ('a bit') is the perfect hedge. This is exceptional economy comedy. 'I hear it a bit' is a highly quotable line.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I like to think of them as my viewers. People who tune in regularly to watch me work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: They're perverts, Ted.

jokes.text: David: 'This isn't one of those animal rights viral videos where the bunnies go through a meat processor in the end, is it?'
jokes.setup: Alexis shows David the bunny cam she set up at Ted's clinic
jokes.punchline: David's immediate assumption of the darkest possible scenario — slaughterhouse videos — reveals both his cynicism and his social media literacy
jokes.explanation: David's dark reading of the situation is funny and character-appropriate — he's been on the internet long enough to imagine worst cases. The specificity of 'meat processor' is darker and therefore funnier than a vague concern would be.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm going to keep the webcam running. I mean, I have nothing to hide. Well, I have some things to hide, but nothing that would be of interest to anyone watching.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Although, I do need to establish some boundaries with my mother about when she can call.

jokes.text: 'Okay, my account is private, thanks.'
jokes.setup: Alexis says 200 viewers is double David's Instagram following
jokes.punchline: David defensively explains his account is private — as if that's why his numbers are low, not because he's simply not popular
jokes.explanation: David's defensive private-account excuse is perfectly in character — he'd rather claim exclusivity than admit low relevance. Short, sharp, and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you haven't even read the script yet.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, I don't need to read a script. I need to read a contract. And a call sheet. And some very flattering behind-the-scenes photography.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But you don't know anything about the role.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I know it's going to be transformative. I can feel it in my soul. This is my moment, David. This is everything I've been working toward.

jokes.text: 'Ted is taking his clothes off.' / 'You wish, David.' / 'No, Ted is getting naked on your webcam!'
jokes.setup: David and Alexis are watching the bunny cam; Ted appears
jokes.punchline: David alerts Alexis that Ted is stripping on camera; Alexis's initial dismissal ('You wish, David') is immediately contradicted by the reality
jokes.explanation: A well-structured setup-punchline with a great mid-beat. 'You wish, David' is the false resolution that makes the actual confirmation land harder. The structure is clean and the escalation is satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It says here that you'll be required to pronounce words with the letter 'X' in them.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's not a problem. I'm quite good with my consonants.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: 'X' is not a consonant, Mother. It's a cry for help.

jokes.text: 'I emailed our entire client list about this, David! I emailed his parents!'
jokes.setup: Ted is unknowingly stripping on the live bunny cam that Alexis promoted
jokes.punchline: The escalating horror: not just clients, but his PARENTS were sent the link to watch
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'clients' to 'his parents' is a perfect cringe comedy beat. Each step up the ladder of embarrassment lands harder. 'I emailed his parents!' is the perfect topper — it crosses a line that makes the horror feel personal and irreversible.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It says here that my accommodations include a cot in the storage room and a mini-fridge that hasn't worked since 2003.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, David, that's what you get for working in the family business.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A cot in a storage room isn't an accommodation, Moira. It's a cry for help.

jokes.text: 'I'm obsessed with this.' — David watching Ted strip on the bunny cam
jokes.setup: Alexis is panicking about the accidental nude stream
jokes.punchline: David, rather than helping, declares himself 'obsessed' — delighted by the chaos
jokes.explanation: David's complete failure to help and active enjoyment of the crisis is a great character beat. 'I'm obsessed with this' is short, perfectly timed, and reveals character. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, it says here that I have to sign a death waiver.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, yes. It's a standard clause.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A death waiver? For what, exactly?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: In case you die.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: In case I die? During what? A fitting?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, one never knows, David.

jokes.text: 'Ted looks good.' / 'Yeah, he does.' / 'David, he looks really good.' / 'Okay, that's gross.'
jokes.setup: David and Alexis are watching Ted undress on camera while Alexis is theoretically in a relationship with him
jokes.punchline: They both agree Ted looks good, but the exchange becomes awkward when Alexis's enthusiasm prompts David's 'that's gross' — the sibling discomfort of appreciating your sister's boyfriend
jokes.explanation: A beautifully awkward sibling exchange. David conceding 'yeah, he does' and then recoiling at Alexis's escalation ('he looks REALLY good') captures the exact discomfort of accidentally bonding with your sibling over their partner's attractiveness. The 'okay, that's gross' boundary-setting lands perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, there's also a clause here about non-compete agreements, and it says that I'm not allowed to—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Stop.

jokes.text: Johnny and Stevie knock on the door and awkwardly check in on the rooms — implicitly confronting whoever is inside
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie go to investigate the rooms they suspect are being used for sex work
jokes.punchline: The reaction shot of the door opening and whoever is inside — Johnny's immediate pivot to 'just wanted to come by and check on you' is a masterclass in avoidance
jokes.explanation: The cringe of Johnny's overly polite confrontation — 'multiple room stay' and 'mature, if not adult activities' — is good but plays out over several lines. The comedy is sustained discomfort as Johnny tries to be both a businessman and a moralist.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, John, you're like a... you're like a baked potato that's been left out in the sun too long, and then someone puts it back in the oven, but they forget to take the foil off, so it's all steamed and weird, but... but in a good way. You know? You're doing great, buddy.

jokes.text: 'And?' — Trish's response to Johnny's accusation
jokes.setup: Johnny has just confronted Trish about adults engaging in 'mature activities' in the motel rooms
jokes.punchline: Trish's single-word 'And?' — completely unfazed, treating his concern as irrelevant
jokes.explanation: One word. Maximum impact. 'And?' deflates Johnny's entire moral confrontation — it's so casual and so complete. The economy score is perfect. Trish's world-weary professionalism vs. Johnny's moralizing is captured in a single syllable. This is exceptional economy comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have no idea what you just said, and I'm not going to pretend that I do.

jokes.text: 'People are making love in these rooms, whether they rent by the day, the week, or the hour.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to shut down the operation as morally compromising
jokes.punchline: Trish's philosophical reframe — people have always been doing this in motel rooms — renders his moral objection moot with irrefutable logic
jokes.explanation: A perfect pragmatist rebuttal delivered by Trish. The rhythm of 'by the day, the week, or the hour' gives it rhetorical weight, and 'making love' as her euphemism is an interesting mirror to Johnny's euphemisms. Philosophically funny and well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm Johnny Rose, I don't have feelings. *makes exaggerated closing gesture* Whoooosh!

jokes.text: Johnny's 'Yeah... yeah, well... point taken. Wow.' — completely deflated by Trish's argument
jokes.setup: Trish has just made an unanswerable philosophical point about motel rooms
jokes.punchline: Johnny's complete capitulation — 'point taken. Wow.' — he can't argue with it
jokes.explanation: Johnny's stunned 'Wow' is a great reaction beat — a former mogul who thought he was going to take the moral high ground being completely outmaneuvered by a calm, pragmatic sex worker. His humiliation is gentle but complete.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Ken's not a client, he's a friend. He comes by to visit.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, he's paying you, isn't he?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: He's paying for the room! That doesn't make him a... that makes him a guest at my motel, you absolute cabbage.

jokes.text: 'Oh. / Oh no, why didn't you tell me the camera could see me when I get back from my runs?!' — Ted's slowly dawning horror
jokes.setup: Alexis tells Ted that the camera can see him 'after his run'
jokes.punchline: Ted processes this in real time — first confused ('Oh.'), then mortified ('Oh no') — the slow comprehension is the joke
jokes.explanation: The two-beat 'Oh.' then 'Oh no' as Ted processes his accidental nudity stream is a good reaction beat. The gap between the two responses implies a silent moment of comprehension that the audience can enjoy before he verbalizes it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You listen here. I'm the mayor of this town, and I have half a mind to shut this whole operation down myself.

jokes.text: Ted's dawning 'Oh.' — a long, slow realization of what the camera saw
jokes.setup: Alexis has just implied the camera saw Ted undressing
jokes.punchline: Ted's single 'Oh.' — a beat of silence as the magnitude of the situation lands — is the punchline
jokes.explanation: The single 'Oh.' is a classic reaction beat — everything that needs to happen happens in the pause before and after it. Ted's performance in this moment does all the work, and the transcript captures it beautifully in one syllable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm gonna call the mayor and tell him what's going on here. And if he doesn't do anything about it, I'm gonna call the mayor of the next town over.

jokes.text: 'Although there was a moment last night, where it looked like you might have known, and you were doing this like, thing with your hips.'
jokes.setup: Alexis assures Ted he couldn't have known he was on camera
jokes.punchline: She immediately contradicts herself by describing a moment where it DID look like he knew — with a hip thing — implying possibly intentional exhibitionism
jokes.explanation: Alexis's self-undermining reassurance is beautifully timed — she's trying to comfort Ted but can't help describing the compromising detail that opens the door to 'maybe he knew.' The 'thing with your hips' is specific enough to be genuinely funny.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, Johnny, if you're going to consort with that type of woman, the least you could do is have a conversation with her about her life choices and morality.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I'm not going to lecture a sex worker about her career.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that's the problem with your generation. No moral compass.

jokes.text: 'People were very concerned as to whether or not you would get your pants on successfully.'
jokes.setup: Ted asks if the website spike might have been people watching the bunnies
jokes.punchline: Alexis clarifies: viewers were specifically invested in whether he'd manage to get his pants on — not the bunnies
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'whether or not you would get your pants on successfully' as an object of concern is wonderfully absurd. It frames the viewers as invested in a specific outcome that implies they were watching intently. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm returning to the duet because I've come to realize that not every artist needs a massive platform to make an impact. Sometimes the most meaningful performances happen in intimate venues where you can really connect with your audience on a deeper level. Plus, I've always believed that true artistry transcends geography. A Rose by any other name would smell as sweet, and a Moira by any other spotlight would still shine just as brightly. Besides, David mentioned something about tax write-offs, and I think we should all be supporting local business.

jokes.text: Ted decides to keep the bunny cam running: 'who's to say that people weren't watching for the bunnies?'
jokes.setup: Alexis just confirmed people were watching Ted get dressed, not the bunnies
jokes.punchline: Ted immediately pivots to self-delusion: maybe it was the bunnies — making a transparent rationalization for keeping the accidental nude cam running
jokes.explanation: Ted's complete character reversal — from outraged moralist to self-deluding exhibitionist — plays as a wonderful escalation of his earlier vanity jokes. 'Who's to say that people weren't watching for the bunnies?' when Alexis literally just told him is perfectly in denial.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your father and I were in the Caribbean, on a boat, and we were listening to 'Islands in the Stream,' and well... let's just say that's how David came to be.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, I don't need to hear this.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We were docked in Bermuda at the time, so technically you were conceived in Bermuda.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Please stop talking.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Though your father insists it was the Bahamas.

jokes.text: Ted decides: 'I feel that until these guys are adopted, we can't take it down, that'd be bad for business, for the bunnies' sake.'
jokes.setup: Ted is rationalizing keeping the webcam that shows him undressing
jokes.punchline: He frames his vanity entirely in terms of the bunnies' welfare — 'for the bunnies' sake' is the perfect fake-selfless kicker
jokes.explanation: A beautiful callback reversal — Ted was worried the bunnies would feel exploited; now he's using the bunnies as cover for his own exhibitionism. The phrase 'for the bunnies' sake' is the perfect ironic cherry. High score earned for the character contradiction and callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Islands in the stream, that is what we are...

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No one in between, how can we be wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Bzzzzt, flamingo, pterodactyl...

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Sparkle, hamster, encyclopedia!

jokes.text: 'Um hello, who are you, and what did you do with Theodore Mullins?'
jokes.setup: Ted has just revealed he's happy to keep being accidentally filmed post-workout
jokes.punchline: Alexis's mock-startled question — treating his new exhibitionist persona as a different person entirely
jokes.explanation: A familiar comedy device (who ARE you?) but deployed with good timing and character-specific detail ('Theodore Mullins' — full name). Works as a beat but not especially original.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'd like to join the duet performance.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Amore, amore, amore! *singing dramatically in Italian* Non, non, non! *switching to French with exaggerated vibrato* Oui, oui, c'est magnifique!

jokes.text: 'Well, what can I say, sometimes I like to live dangerously. While still respecting my personal boundaries.'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks who this new 'dangerous' Ted is
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'I like to live dangerously' is immediately self-corrected with '...while still respecting my personal boundaries' — the hedge makes the bravado collapse
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'I like to live dangerously. While still respecting my personal boundaries.' is a perfect character encapsulation — Ted is fundamentally a rule-follower who can't even claim to be wild without immediately qualifying it. The self-correction is the joke, and it captures him completely. Highly quotable, rewatch-worthy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ahhhhh, mon dieu! I simply must— *joins in with elaborate operatic flourishes in mock French and Italian*

jokes.text: 'You booked it.' — David reading the email to Moira
jokes.setup: Moira has been anxiously waiting to hear from the film
jokes.punchline: David reads the subject line and delivers the good news flatly — setting up what will quickly become bad news
jokes.explanation: The setup beat for a much better joke — the news is good for one second before conditions begin to emerge. Functional but not a standalone joke.

jokes.text: The film is being shot 'in a city I don't know the name of... a lot of consonants'
jokes.setup: David reads the filming location details
jokes.punchline: He can't pronounce the city — 'a lot of consonants' — a genteel way to dismiss Eastern European geography
jokes.explanation: David's helpless 'a lot of consonants' is a good character-specific way to convey geographical ignorance. In character for someone who would never have thought about Bosnia before.

jokes.text: David reads the shooting location: 'It's filming in Bosnia! Um, in a city that I don't, I don't know the name of the city, I can't pronounce it, a lot of consonants.'
jokes.setup: The movie deal seems to be coming together
jokes.punchline: The location is in Bosnia, and David can't pronounce the city name — a string of consonants representing an unglamorous shooting location
jokes.explanation: The slow reveal of the terms — starting with Bosnia — is the first sign that this isn't the prestige gig Moira imagined. David's description ('a lot of consonants') is a nice character-specific way to convey geographic unfamiliarity.

jokes.text: 'I did not know that. But I've always loved shooting on location, it allows one to focus on the work.'
jokes.setup: David reveals the film is shooting in Bosnia
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately pivots to artistic rationale — 'shooting on location allows one to focus on the work' — pure spin from someone who is clearly now worried
jokes.explanation: Moira's rapid reframe of bad news as artistic opportunity is in perfect character. 'It allows one to focus on the work' is convincing for about one second before more bad news arrives.

jokes.text: The film is being released in Bosnia, Croatia, Herzegovina — 'before opening internationally!'
jokes.setup: The film's distribution is being read out
jokes.punchline: 'Before opening internationally!' — Moira/David note the very limited Balkan release as if a wider international release is definitely coming
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the framing of a tiny regional release as a 'before' — as if international domination is the natural next step from Bosnia/Croatia/Herzegovina. A wry escalation in the contract revelation sequence.

jokes.text: 'You have to fly yourself there?' / 'Mmm, that doesn't sound right.'
jokes.setup: The contract details continue to deteriorate
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'that doesn't sound right' — denial in the face of a clearly written contract — as if contracts can have typos that unfavorable to actresses
jokes.explanation: Moira's denial of a clearly unfavorable contract term ('that doesn't sound right') is funny and in character — she's been suppressing bad news with optimism, and now she's actively refusing reality.

jokes.text: David: 'You have to fly yourself there?' Moira: 'Mmm, that doesn't sound right.'
jokes.setup: The terms of the deal are being read aloud
jokes.punchline: No travel is covered — Moira must fly herself to Bosnia. Moira's response: 'That doesn't sound right.' — as if there's been a clerical error
jokes.explanation: Moira's 'that doesn't sound right' — the passive refusal to accept obvious reality — is perfectly in character. The reading of the terms becomes a comedic countdown of indignities.

jokes.text: 'Ooh, accommodation is covered.' / 'Thank God!' / 'You'll be put up with a local family.'
jokes.setup: The accommodation being covered sounds like relief after the self-financed travel
jokes.punchline: 'You'll be put up with a local family' — not a hotel, a local Bosnian family's home
jokes.explanation: A beautifully timed misdirection. 'Accommodation is covered' → 'Thank God!' → 'You'll be put up with a local family.' The escalating deflation of Moira's hopes reaches a great nadir here. The beat timing is perfect. Moira's 'Thank God!' makes the reveal funnier.

jokes.text: 'They're paying you scale.' / 'But it'll be in Baltic currency.'
jokes.setup: Moira hopes to at least be paid
jokes.punchline: She's getting scale — minimum — in Baltic currency, which is presumably worth very little
jokes.explanation: Another layer of the bad-deal revelation — minimum wage in a currency Moira has never encountered. The comedic escalation of the contract details continues to build effectively.

jokes.text: 'There's a death waiver?!'
jokes.setup: The contract terms have been getting progressively worse
jokes.punchline: The ultimate escalation — a death waiver, implying working conditions dangerous enough to require signing away liability for death
jokes.explanation: The perfect topper to the escalating contract disaster. After travel costs, accommodation with strangers, scale in Baltic currency, David finds a death waiver. The escalation structure is excellent and this is the comedic peak of the sequence. 'There's a death waiver?!' delivered as genuine shock with the exclamation is highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'You can close the computer.' — Moira's response to the death waiver
jokes.setup: David has just found a death waiver in the contract
jokes.punchline: Moira's quiet 'You can close the computer' — the death waiver is the thing that finally makes her give up, delivered with total composure
jokes.explanation: The episode's best Moira joke. Five words delivered with perfect Moira composure — she's survived the travel costs, the local family accommodation, the Baltic currency, and she hits the death waiver and simply says 'You can close the computer.' The understatement is everything. It captures Moira's dignity in the face of humiliation and is the perfect button to a great escalating sequence. Economy score: perfect.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, but we haven't even gotten to the section on bird safety yet!'
jokes.setup: Moira has just asked David to close the computer after the death waiver
jokes.punchline: David reveals there's more — specifically a 'section on bird safety' — implying the crows themselves are a documented workplace hazard
jokes.explanation: A perfect topper to the topper. Moira has already given up, and David reveals there's a SECTION on bird safety — not a clause, a whole section — which means the bird hazard is substantial enough to warrant extended contractual treatment. The absurdity is perfectly calibrated and David's gleeful continuation as Moira shuts down is excellent. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: 'Shut it! Now.' — Moira's final word on the laptop
jokes.setup: David has revealed a bird safety section exists in the contract
jokes.punchline: Moira goes from composed ('You can close the computer') to direct ('Shut it! Now.') — a crack in the composure
jokes.explanation: A callback beat — Moira having to escalate her laptop-closing instructions because David won't let it go. The transition from dignified to sharp works as a comedic release.

jokes.text: Moira's final decision: 'What if...' (pause) 'Hmm. No, thank you.'
jokes.setup: After the death waiver and bird safety section are revealed, Moira contemplates the offer
jokes.punchline: She briefly considers something — perhaps talking herself into it — then simply: 'No, thank you.' The theatrical consideration followed by a quiet decline
jokes.explanation: The comedy of Moira's decline is in what isn't said — she considers, pauses, then declines with perfect politeness. The 'No, thank you' is absurdly gentle for turning down a contract that includes a death waiver. The pause does enormous work. Character-perfect and economical.

jokes.text: Roland's metaphor: 'when I first heard that Stevie was hitching her wagon to your cart, the first thing that came into my mind was, whoooa, Nelly! That horse is in big trouble.'
jokes.setup: Roland offers Johnny a backhanded compliment about his business partnership with Stevie
jokes.punchline: Roland's convoluted metaphor mixing wagon, cart, and a separate horse ('Nelly') — a jumbled endorsement that also insults
jokes.explanation: Roland's mixed metaphor is a perfectly calibrated character joke — he's trying to be sophisticated and complimentary but just can't keep his imagery straight. 'A convoluted metaphor' is Johnny's own acknowledgment of it. Strong character integration for Roland.

jokes.text: Johnny's verbal tic impression: 'Ooh Roland, my generation, we don't talk about our feelings, we keep 'em all rawrrr inside!'
jokes.setup: Johnny deflects Roland's attempt to have a genuine moment
jokes.punchline: Roland does an impression of Johnny's emotional repression, complete with a sound effect ('rawrrr') for suppressed feelings
jokes.explanation: Roland doing an impression of Johnny's emotional buttoning-up — with a sound effect for the internal suppression — is a great Roland bit. The 'rawrrr' is the specific comedic touch that elevates it.

jokes.text: Kitty doesn't know what Ken looks like — 'she doesn't know what he looks like?'
jokes.setup: A woman at the motel asks if either man is 'Ken' — someone she apparently can't identify by sight
jokes.punchline: Roland clocks immediately that a woman who can't pick out 'Ken' by sight is not meeting a spouse — she's a client
jokes.explanation: Roland's instant understanding of the situation — recognizing that a woman who doesn't know what 'Ken' looks like is working — is a good joke. It also makes him inadvertently complicit in explaining the situation to Johnny.

jokes.text: 'Oh, Johnny.' — Roland's amused reaction after seeing Kitty leave a room
jokes.setup: Johnny has been insisting the motel operation is innocent; Kitty walks out of a room
jokes.punchline: Roland's two-word 'Oh, Johnny' carries the weight of complete understanding — the look says everything
jokes.explanation: Two words used to maximum effect — Roland's affectionate, knowing 'Oh, Johnny' closes the chapter of Johnny's denial. The economy is excellent and it's completely in character for Roland to use warmth rather than judgment.

jokes.text: Roland's reaction after the penny drops: '(Laughs) Oh, Johnny.'
jokes.setup: Roland has just realized what kind of operation Johnny has unknowingly been running
jokes.punchline: His laughter and 'Oh, Johnny' — the laugh of someone who finds their friend's moral downfall delightful
jokes.explanation: Chris Elliott's delivery of Roland's gleeful 'Oh, Johnny' would be the joke — the schadenfreude of a man who finally has something on his usually superior neighbor. Competent character beat.

jokes.text: 'I would hate to have to assert my mayoral authority in a situation like this.'
jokes.setup: Roland has caught Johnny running what amounts to a sex motel
jokes.punchline: Roland's invocation of his 'mayoral authority' — as if being mayor of Schitt's Creek carries meaningful gravitas — to resolve a sex work situation
jokes.explanation: Roland's pompous invocation of mayoral authority in a small town context is funny — the disconnect between the title and its actual power is a consistent show joke. Works here as a specific character beat.

jokes.text: 'Well, I'm glad I helped you fix your uh, broken moral compass.' / 'Well, there's nothing wrong with my moral compass, but...' / 'All right, well, why don't we tell Kitty that?'
jokes.setup: Johnny insists he was going to shut the operation down anyway
jokes.punchline: Roland diagnoses Johnny's 'broken moral compass' — which Johnny indignantly rejects — and Roland immediately offers to verify with Kitty the sex worker who just left
jokes.explanation: Roland's offer to have Kitty adjudicate Johnny's moral compass is a good topper — the absurdity of calling on a sex worker as a character reference is the joke. Works well.

jokes.text: 'While the Bosnian Riviera is a majestic locale, it can't hold a candle to singing... with you wonderful working women here, in our makeshift rehearsal studio.'
jokes.setup: Moira returns to the duet rehearsal after turning down the film
jokes.punchline: She describes a community center as a 'makeshift rehearsal studio' and invokes 'the Bosnian Riviera' as what she's giving up — implying Bosnia has a glamorous Riviera
jokes.explanation: Moira has reframed her humiliating rejection of a terrible deal as a noble choice to prioritize community. 'The Bosnian Riviera' is a particularly funny invention — she's upgraded a filming location in an unnamed consonant-heavy city to a glamorous coast. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'John and I made David to that song, in Pusitano.'
jokes.setup: Moira is arguing that 'Islands in the Stream' is particularly meaningful to her
jokes.punchline: Her reason: the song is the soundtrack to David's conception — delivered completely casually to a group of acquaintances, in front of David
jokes.explanation: A great Moira overshare delivered with total serenity. Revealing that 'Islands in the Stream' is significant because that's the song to which she and John conceived David — told casually to a group, without any acknowledgment of David's presence or feelings — is perfectly Moira. 'In Pusitano' as a specific location (implying a glamorous Italian destination) adds to the joke. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Jocelyn and Twyla perform 'Islands in the Stream' with Twyla scatting 'K-H-E-O-K!' in place of actual lyrics
jokes.setup: The women perform the duet they've been rehearsing
jokes.punchline: Twyla's contribution to the song is 'K-H-E-O-K!' — completely meaningless syllables delivered with full commitment, apparently in place of the actual Kenny Rogers/Dolly Parton line
jokes.explanation: The visual (and audio) comedy of Twyla confidently scatting 'K-H-E-O-K!' as if those are real words in the song is genuinely funny. The commitment to wrong lyrics delivered right is a staple comedy technique, and the specificity of those particular sounds elevates it.

jokes.text: Moira sings loudly in another language over the performance — her way of 'offering professional advice'
jokes.setup: Moira was told she could 'offer professional advice' while Twyla and Jocelyn perform
jokes.punchline: Her 'professional advice' is to begin belting a different version in what sounds like another language, drowning out the actual performers
jokes.explanation: Moira cannot watch a performance without inserting herself. Her 'professional advice' becoming a competing vocal performance in another language is a perfect character joke — her ego physically cannot stay silent. Strong character integration and a great visual/audio comedy closer for the episode.

tmdb_overview: Johnny puts the motel in jeopardy when he rents rooms to a dubious enterprise and David helps Moira with a film audition.
insight: David's interior design obsession drives 18 of 60 jokes, anchoring the episode's satirical backbone.
